figures.caption: Figure 4 Joint optimization analysis. Left: Time–Energy Pareto frontier annotated with ρ.
Table 4 Energy coefficients and power profiles (normalized units). Computation power follows Pcomp = κ(µc)3, where a lower κ indicates higher hardware efficiency.
Figure 5 Empirical Time-Energy Pareto frontier for the KMNIST dataset, illustrating the mean clock-time versus mean energy required to reach a target test accuracy of 0.75 across 10 independent runs.

Figure 2 Impact of concurrency m and routing probability p1 on E0[τϵ]. Left: Homogeneous resources. Right: Heterogeneous resources, where Client 2 is three times faster than Client 1 (µs 2 = 3µs 1 for s ∈ {c, u, d}). Constants: ∆ = L = σ = 1, M = 5, and G = 14.

Figure 3 Test set performance at the CS for the scenario in Section 5.3.1. The top row displays test accuracy and the bottom row shows loss, both plotted against wall-clock time. Columns correspond to different service time distributions under homogeneous and heterogeneous data settings. Each subplot compares the four strategies. Simulations were repeated 10 times for 400 wall-clock time units. Solid lines indicate means; shaded areas represent standard deviations.

Table 5 Relative time and energy reduction (%) of (p∗ρ, m∗ρ) at ρ = 0.1 compared to AsyncSGD at target accuracies of 0.80 (KMNIST) and 0.70 (EMNIST). Positive values indicate savings; negative denote increases.
Table 8 Percentage time reduction of the Time-optimized strategy relative to baselines for a target test accuracy of 0.5 on CIFAR-100.
Table 7 Comparison of optimized routing probabilities and staleness impact factors across different client clusters (Types A–E).
Figure 1 Queueing model with computation times and communication delays.
Table 3 Percentage time reduction relative to baselines for a target test accuracy of 0.6 (0.75 in parentheses).

Figure 7 Test set accuracy and loss vs. rounds for the EMNIST dataset (n = 100, m = n) using the client profiles defined in Table 6. The subplots evaluate performance under three service time distributions: (Top Left) Exponential, (Top Right) Lognormal, and (Bottom) Deterministic. Each plot compares convergence across three data settings: Homogeneous (IID), Heterogeneous (Dirichlet α = 0.2), and Highly Heterogeneous (3 classes/client).
Figure 6 Extended queueing network model incorporating computation, communication, and

Figure 8 Optimized expected time to ϵ-accuracy, E0[τϵ](p∗, m), as a function of the concurrency level m. The curve illustrates the fundamental trade-off between parallel speedup and gradient staleness, with the inset highlighting the minimum achieved at the optimal concurrency m∗ = 91.

Figure 9 Test set performance for the CIFAR-100 scenario described in Section 5.3.1. The top row displays test accuracy and the bottom row shows loss, both plotted against wall-clock time. Columns correspond to different service time distributions under homogeneous (IID) and heterogeneous (Non-IID) data settings. Each subplot compares the three strategies. Simulations were repeated 3 times for 6000 wall-clock time units. Solid lines indicate means; shaded areas represent standard deviations.
Table 2 Comparison of optimized routing probabilities and staleness impact factors across different client clusters (Types A–E).

Figure 10 Learning trajectories on the EMNIST dataset under the experimental scenario detailed in Section 5.3.1 and the power profile from Table 4. The top and bottom rows display test accuracy as a function of total energy consumption and wall-clock time, respectively. Columns represent different service time distributions across homogeneous (IID) and heterogeneous (Non-IID) data partitions. Each panel compares the convergence dynamics of the five routing strategies. Results are averaged over 10 independent runs spanning 300,000 energy units and 300 time units, with solid lines indicating the mean and shaded regions representing the standard deviation.
